title: Tapinanthus sankuruensis
taxonomy: Tapinanthus sankuruensis is a species of plant in the family Santalaceae. This plant is a type of mistletoe, a hemiparasitic flowering plant that obtains some of its nutrients by parasitizing the tissues of other plants. The classification of Tapinanthus sankuruensis is further broken down into the kingdom Plantae, and the class Magnoliopsida. Tapinanthus sankuruensis is a member of the order Santalales and the family Santalaceae. The genus Tapinanthus is a group of mistletoes found primarily in Africa.

The plant has been traditionally used in some African cultures to treat various ailments, including fever, pain, and inflammation. intro: Tapinanthus sankuruensis, also known as the "Angola dwarf mistletoe," is a parasitic plant species belonging to the family Loranthaceae. This plant has been traditionally used in African folk medicine, boasting potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may aid in the treatment of various conditions including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Tapinanthus sankuruensis include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and also used in the form of ointments and tinctures to leverage its therapeutic benefits.
